skill.name: rare-disease-hpo-mapper
skill.description: Map patient symptoms to Human Phenotype Ontology terms for gene diagnosis.
skill.summary: Convert clinical symptom descriptions into standardized HPO ontology terms with confidence scoring for genetic diagnosis workflows.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 46 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 30 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown inline code spans (backticks used for formatting, not command injection). The 4 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings matched on unrelated text in JSON metadata and SKILL.md documentation. The 4 'system reconnaissance' findings matched on assertion notes and output formatting text. The 2 'path traversal' findings are from a security checklist documenting what to avoid, not actual traversal code. The hardcoded URL is a legitimate GitHub source attribution. The packaged script (scripts/main.py) is a simple, transparent CLI tool using Python difflib for fuzzy symptom matching with no network, file I/O, or dangerous patterns.

content.user_title: Map Rare Disease Symptoms to HPO Terms for Genetic Diagnosis
content.value_statement: Clinicians and researchers need to convert patient symptom descriptions into standardized Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO) terms for genetic analysis. This skill automates the mapping process using exact and fuzzy matching, returning HPO term IDs with confidence scores to support structured diagnosis workflows.
actual_capabilities: Matches patient symptom descriptions to HPO terms using exact text and fuzzy string matching
Returns HPO term IDs with standardized names and confidence scores for each mapping
Processes comma-separated symptom lists in a single batch operation
Identifies unmatched symptoms clearly for manual review and follow-up
Provides a lightweight offline mapping tool using Python standard library with difflib
limitations: Includes only a small built-in HPO term set (6 terms), not the full ontology
Fuzzy matching uses basic string similarity, not clinical NLP or semantic understanding
Does not perform gene prioritization or differential diagnosis from HPO terms
Requires symptom descriptions as plain text input, not structured medical records

use_cases.title: Standardize patient symptoms for variant interpretation
use_cases.description: Convert free-text clinical findings into HPO terms before running exome or genome analysis pipelines.

use_cases.title: Harmonize phenotype data across research cohorts
use_cases.description: Map symptom descriptions from multiple sources to a common ontology for cohort building and statistical analysis.

use_cases.title: Document structured phenotypes for genetic counseling
use_cases.description: Capture observed symptoms in standardized ontology format during family history and pedigree analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Map a single symptom to HPO
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the rare-disease-hpo-mapper skill to find the HPO term for this symptom: seizure
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user wanting to map one clinical finding to its HPO code

prompt_templates.title: Map multiple symptoms in batch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the rare-disease-hpo-mapper skill to map these symptoms to HPO terms: seizures, short stature, developmental delay, cataract
prompt_templates.scenario: User with a list of patient findings that need standardized ontology coding

prompt_templates.title: Review and verify unmapped symptoms
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the rare-disease-hpo-mapper skill on these symptoms: ataxia, cardiomyopathy, nystagmus. For any unmatched terms, suggest alternative symptom descriptions that might produce a match.
prompt_templates.scenario: User who needs to handle partial matches and refine input for better coverage

prompt_templates.title: Integrate HPO mapping into analysis workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the rare-disease-hpo-mapper skill to process these symptoms: seizures, cardiomyopathy, short stature. Then organize the output into a structured table showing the symptom, HPO ID, HPO name, and confidence for each matched term. List unmatched terms separately.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user needing structured output for downstream analysis or documentation

output_examples.input: Map these symptoms: seizures, short stature, heart muscle disease
output_examples.output:
✓ Seizures → HP:0001250 (Seizure)
✓ Short stature → HP:0004322 (Short stature)
✓ Heart muscle disease → HP:0001638 (Cardiomyopathy)

3 of 3 symptoms matched successfully.

output_examples.input: Map: developmental delay, nystagmus, coordination problems
output_examples.output:
✓ Developmental delay → HP:0001263 (Global developmental delay)
✗ Nystagmus → No match found
✓ Coordination problems → HP:0001251 (Ataxia)

2 of 3 symptoms matched. 1 symptom needs manual review.

best_practices: Provide specific, standard clinical symptom descriptions rather than vague terms for the best matching results
Review unmatched symptoms manually and try alternative standard terminology that may exist in the ontology
Verify all automated HPO term assignments against clinical findings before using them for diagnosis
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to replace clinical diagnosis or medical decision-making by a qualified professional
Do not rely solely on automated HPO mapping without expert review of assigned ontology terms
Do not input protected health information without proper de-identification and consent

faq.question: What is the Human Phenotype Ontology?
faq.answer: The Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO) is a standardized vocabulary of phenotypic abnormalities encountered in human disease. It provides a structured way to describe clinical findings for genetic analysis.

faq.question: How many HPO terms are included in the mapper?
faq.answer: The mapper includes 6 built-in HPO terms. For full ontology coverage, you need to extend the HPO_TERMS dictionary in the script or integrate with an external HPO database.

faq.question: What happens if my symptom does not match any term?
faq.answer: Unmatched symptoms are reported with a clear indicator and zero confidence. You should review them manually and try alternative standard descriptions.

faq.question: Does this skill make network calls?
faq.answer: No. The mapping is performed locally using string matching and Python difflib. No external APIs or network requests are made during execution.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for clinical diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. This skill is a research and documentation aid. All mappings must be verified by a qualified clinician before use in diagnosis or treatment decisions.

faq.question: What output format does the skill produce?
faq.answer: The skill produces a text report showing each input symptom, its matching HPO ID and name, plus a confidence indicator. Unmatched symptoms are clearly marked for follow-up.
